Hi Sara — thanks again for the demo this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum: 

1) Quickstart scope (what we propose to provision for Clearspring trial):
   - Hosted sandbox endpoints (streaming + non-streaming)
   - 2 sandbox API keys, 30-day trial credits, and a per-endpoint rate slice for your integration tests
   - Prefix/KV prefetch guidance for conversational flows (we’ll share example configs)

2) Clarifying questions we need from you to size the sandbox properly: 
   - Typical requests/sec during peak and steady state (TPS), and expected burst behavior
   - Median input tokens and expected generated tokens per call (helps pick model/endpoint)
   - Any regulatory constraints (data residency / retention / log access) for test data

3) Proposed next steps / timeline:
   - You share the sample traffic profile (CSV fine) — I’ll pass it to Monica for capacity mapping
   - We provision sandbox + keys within 48 hours of receipt
   - Kickoff call to confirm endpoint mapping and test plan (30 mins)

Attachments: quickstart-map-20280421.pdf (ops map), sample-traffic-profile.csv (blank template)

If this looks good I’ll provision the trial and copy Monica to run the performance mapping. Do you have availability Tuesday or Wednesday for a short kickoff?

Thanks,
Jordan
Jordan Blake
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123



From: Sara Lim <sara@clearspring.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 21 Apr 2028 11:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted API quick trial — endpoint map & next steps

Jordan — great recap, thanks. A few answers and one ask from our side: 

- Traffic: steady 5 TPS across our critical endpoint, expected daily spikes up to ~18–22 TPS for ~5–10m windows (campaign bursts).
- Tokens: average payload ~350 input tokens, we expect ~600–900 generated tokens during longer completions.
- Residency: we must keep production data in the US; for the trial we can use synthetic or redacted samples.
- Security: SOC 2 + evidence required; procurement will request the SOC 2 report and our security questionnaire answers before any paid contract.

We’ll complete the sample-traffic-profile.csv and send back by EOD Monday. For kickoff, Tuesday 9:30–10:00 PDT works for our engineering lead (I copied procurement separately).

Also, does the Hosted offering support private VPC peering if we decide to move away from hosted in production? We’d like to understand migration paths (hosted -> dedicated -> private) and what triggers are typical.

Thanks,
Sara Lim
Head of Platform, Clearspring AI
sara@clearspring.ai



From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 21 Apr 2028 13:05:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Hosted API quick trial — endpoint map & next steps

Monica/Vanessa — forwarding Sara’s reply (traffic profile + SOC 2 ask). Key asks: 5 TPS steady, spikes to 20 TPS, ~350/800 token profile. They want to know migration path options (hosted -> dedicated -> private) and we’ll need to provide SOC2 evidence for procurement. Can you advise: 

- Recommended sandbox endpoint configuration (model family + streaming vs buffered) given the token profile
- Suggested rate slice to provision in sandbox to mirror production bursts (I’m thinking a baseline 10 TPS with burst to 25 TPS for short windows)
- Expected lead time if they require a dedicated capacity path later (rough calendar days)

I’ve attached my quickstart map and the traffic CSV template they’ll return. Planning to confirm provisioning once Monica signs off.

Thanks — Jordan



From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 22 Apr 2028 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Hosted API quick trial — endpoint map & next steps

Thanks for the context. Recommendation based on the sample numbers: 

- Sandbox endpoints: provide one streaming endpoint and one non-streaming endpoint. For Clearspring profile (350 input / 800 output), use a Gen2 medium endpoint (balanced throughput-latency) for initial tests; if they see consistent >10 TPS we upgrade to Gen2 large or shard across two endpoints.
- Rate slice: provision baseline 10 TPS with allowed short-term bursts to 30 TPS (we can enforce burst windows via rate policies). That matches their 20 TPS spikes and gives headroom for tests.
- Model selection: start with an 8-16B open model we’ve validated for streaming; propose quantized variant to lower cost for heavy evals.
- Migration path: Hosted -> Dedicated can be done with a data-plane reservation; typical lead time 2–6 weeks (depending on capacity commitments). Private VPC/on-prem requires security review + deployment package; plan 6–12 weeks for enterprise customers. We’ll surface exact SLA/lead times in a one-pager for procurement.
- SOC2: attach the SOC 2 summary link (I’ll send the signed report through secure portal). For the trial, we can provide redacted evidence and standard questionnaire responses.

If that sounds right I’ll trigger sandbox provisioning once Jordan confirms kickoff time.

Monica Patel
Principal Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
monica.patel@redwood.com | +1 650-555-0199

source.subject: AsterionBio dedicated capacity proposal and throughput safety margin
messages:
From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Hi Emma / Samuel,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — great to understand the mix of short clinical prompts + longer summarization (average response length ~420 tokens). As promised, I attached an initial throughput model (asterion_throughput_model.xlsx) that maps your stated traffic profile to recommended Dedicated capacity and a small burst pool. Quick summary of the model and a couple of questions so we can lock in sizing: 

Key numbers we used (please confirm):
- Average tokens per generation: 420 tokens
- Peak concurrent requests: 120 RPS at peak minute windows (you mentioned a top-of-hour spike pattern)
- Expected p95 response length during spikes: 700 tokens

Redwood runtime assumptions used in the model:
- Model planned to serve: Llama-3-70B (8-bit quantized) with Redwood kernel optimizations
- Measured throughput per 80GB GPU (baseline A100-like profile in our testbed): 130 tokens/sec sustained with small batching (typical 8–16 req batch)
- Target utilization for Dedicated slab sizing: 70% (we leave room for KV cache warmup, background jobs, and scheduling jitter)
- Safety margin (headroom) we recommend: 35% for first 90 days while you ramp and stabilize

Sizing math (short):
- Peak tokens/sec estimate = peak RPS * p95 tokens = 120 * 700 = 84,000 tokens/sec
- Effective tokens/sec per GPU (after util & safety) = 130 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.35) ≈ 59 tokens/sec
- GPUs required (rounded up) = ceil(84,000 / 59) ≈ 1,424 GPUs

That number is intentionally conservative and assumes the heavier summarization workload and a large safety margin. The spreadsheet includes alternate scenarios (smaller safety margin, quantization to 4-bit, caching gains for repeated prefixes) which bring the GPU need down substantially.

Asks / clarifications we need to finalize:
1) Can you confirm the peak minute RPS number (120) and whether the 120 is sustained across a 60s window or a sub-10s microburst?
2) Do you have an expected distribution of token lengths (e.g., % of requests under 128 tokens, 128–512, 512–1,000, >1,000)? That materially affects batching efficiency and KV cache benefits.
3) Any hard p95 latency SLO you must meet for production (e.g., 300ms, 500ms)? Our sizing trades off tokens/sec capacity for lower tail latency.

Next steps: once you confirm (1)–(3) we’ll lock a recommended slab configuration and a proposed commitment tier. I’ve looped Jordan (pricing) and Priyom (inference runtime / ops) to weigh in on tradeoffs — Priyom will propose an optimized configuration that reduces GPU count by applying burst pooling, prefix caching, and quantization recommendations.

Thanks,

Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila_reyes@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference

Attachments: asterion_throughput_model.xlsx (spreadsheet), redwood-dedicated-proposal.pdf (proposal stub)
From: Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Camila — thanks, this is helpful and ambitious. A couple of clarifications from our side: 

- The 120 RPS number is the worst-case top-of-hour spike measured over a 10s window (so it’s a short burst, not sustained for the full minute). We have a broader background of ~18 RPS steady-state.
- Token distribution (rough approximate): ~55% under 256 tokens (short lookups), ~30% in 256–700, ~12% 700–1600 (long summaries), ~3% occasional >1600 when returning long patient history aggregations.
- Our latency SLO: p95 <= 450ms for text-only endpoints. For the long summarization flows we are willing to accept p95 <= 900ms if necessary (the user flow is async).

Also: we are open to hybrid strategies — a Dedicated slab for predictable baseline and a hosted burst pool for those 10s spikes. Curious how you modeled burst pools and warm-start vs. cold starts in the spreadsheet.

Sam
Senior Engineering Manager, AsterionBio
samuel.choi@asterionbio.com

-----Original message-----
On Fri, Apr 20, 2028 at 09:12 AM Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com> wrote:
> (see above)
From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>, Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 11:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Thanks for the details, Sam — that clarifies a lot. A few technical notes and an alternative sizing approach that will reduce the 1,400+ GPU topline estimate substantially: 

1) Burst vs sustained math: because the 120 RPS is a 10s microburst, you don’t need to commit 1,400 GPUs for the whole time window. Two levers help: a) a small dedicated slab sized for the sustained baseline (we’ll size for 18 RPS steady-state), b) a shared hosted burst pool (or a short-term elastic burst slab) that covers the 10s spikes.

2) Effective tokens/sec per GPU by request mix: the 130 tokens/sec baseline we shared assumes long 700-token responses are dominant. With your mix (55% <256), our internal microbench shows an average tokens/sec of ~210 tokens/sec per GPU when batching is efficient across mixed lengths (the shorter requests pack multiple per batch).

3) Recompute with pragmatic headroom:
- Peak tokens/sec instantaneous for 10s burst = 120 RPS * 700 tokens = 84,000 tokens/sec (same as Camila’s calc)
- Sustained baseline tokens/sec = 18 RPS * 420 avg = 7,560 tokens/sec
- Effective per-GPU tokens/sec (mixed profile, 70% util, 25% safety margin) = 210 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.25) ≈ 110 tokens/sec

Sizing recommendation (hybrid):
- Dedicated baseline slab: size to meet sustained baseline with room for steady growth: GPUs_baseline = ceil(7,560 / 110) = 69 GPUs
- Burst coverage (short 10s spikes): incremental GPUs needed = ceil((84,000 - (69 * 210 * 0.70)) / 110) ≈ ceil((84,000 - 10,143) / 110) ≈ 677 GPUs transient.

Operational notes:
- We can satisfy spike coverage by a mix of: (A) hosted burst pool (pay-as-you-go) + (B) short-term refundable burst slab (30–90 day) — both approaches reduce your upfront committed GPUs.
- Additional levers to lower total GPUs: prefix caching (for repeated clinical query patterns), 4-bit quantization (adds ~25–40% tokens/sec per GPU in our tests for this model class), and request-level batching tweaks.

I’ve updated the spreadsheet with scenario tabs for: baseline-only, hybrid-burst, and high-quant. If you want, I can run a stress test in our test cluster with a representative sample payload next week to validate the 210 tokens/sec estimate.

Priyom Das
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
priyom_das@redwood.com

Attachments: none
From: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>, Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 13:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Emma / Sam,

Pricing and contracting note: the hybrid approach Priyom outlines maps well to our "Baseline + Burst" offer. High level:
- Baseline commitment: 12-month term for the 69 GPUs (reserved slab) with standard enterprise SLA and telemetry.
- Burst coverage: credit-based hosted burst pool for short spikes, with an option for a 90-day refundable slab (we reserve the physical capacity but refund if unused beyond a threshold).
- If you opt for heavier quantization + prefix cache optimizations and agree to a 24-month commitment, we can price the baseline at ~14% lower unit cost and add one week of free ramping support.

Emma — if that pricing approach sounds reasonable I’ll route a preliminary SOW and a DocuSign link; we can tie the final GPU counts to the stress-test results Priyom mentioned.

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Head of Sales Operations, Redwood Inference
jordan_blake@redwood.com

Attachment: redwood-dedicated-proposal.pdf (pricing summary)
From: Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 21 Apr 2028 17:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Thanks everyone — the hybrid proposal makes sense and aligns with our procurement constraints. Two quick asks before we move to SOW: 

1) Can Redwood run a 2h stress test next Tuesday (sample traffic profile attached in the spreadsheet tab) to validate the 210 tokens/sec / GPU mixed-profile number? Please include p95 latency logs and a heatmap of batch sizes during the run.
2) Please send a DocuSign SOW with the 12-month 69-GPU baseline + burst credits option so we can start internal approval. Our legal team needs the SLOs and an escalation contact listed.

If you can have both items in the next 48 hours we’ll aim to have an internal sign-off by end of next week.

Emma Jones
VP Engineering, AsterionBio
emma.jones@asterionbio.com

> On Apr 20, 2028, at 11:18 AM, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com> wrote:
> > (see above)

From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 21 Apr 2028 17:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Emma — thanks, will do. Quick confirmation of next steps from our side: 

- Priyom will schedule the 2h stress test for Tue 2028-04-24 at 10:00 PT and will share a test playbook + expected payloads by EOD Monday.
- Jordan will circulate the preliminary SOW / DocuSign for the 12-month 69-GPU baseline + burst credits option (includes the 14% trade price if you choose 24 months).
- I’ll add our escalation contact (L2: priya_ops@redwood.com, L3: jordan_blake@redwood.com) to the SOW.

I attached a slightly updated proposal doc (AST-8421-SOW.docx) with the hybrid tab called out. We’ll keep the upfront committed GPUs conservative and iterate after the stress test — this should keep your capital exposure low while giving strong protection for p95 latency.

Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila_reyes@redwood.com

Attachments: AST-8421-SOW.docx (SOW draft)

text:
From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Hi Emma / Samuel,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — great to understand the mix of short clinical prompts + longer summarization (average response length ~420 tokens). As promised, I attached an initial throughput model (asterion_throughput_model.xlsx) that maps your stated traffic profile to recommended Dedicated capacity and a small burst pool. Quick summary of the model and a couple of questions so we can lock in sizing: 

Key numbers we used (please confirm):
- Average tokens per generation: 420 tokens
- Peak concurrent requests: 120 RPS at peak minute windows (you mentioned a top-of-hour spike pattern)
- Expected p95 response length during spikes: 700 tokens

Redwood runtime assumptions used in the model:
- Model planned to serve: Llama-3-70B (8-bit quantized) with Redwood kernel optimizations
- Measured throughput per 80GB GPU (baseline A100-like profile in our testbed): 130 tokens/sec sustained with small batching (typical 8–16 req batch)
- Target utilization for Dedicated slab sizing: 70% (we leave room for KV cache warmup, background jobs, and scheduling jitter)
- Safety margin (headroom) we recommend: 35% for first 90 days while you ramp and stabilize

Sizing math (short):
- Peak tokens/sec estimate = peak RPS * p95 tokens = 120 * 700 = 84,000 tokens/sec
- Effective tokens/sec per GPU (after util & safety) = 130 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.35) ≈ 59 tokens/sec
- GPUs required (rounded up) = ceil(84,000 / 59) ≈ 1,424 GPUs

That number is intentionally conservative and assumes the heavier summarization workload and a large safety margin. The spreadsheet includes alternate scenarios (smaller safety margin, quantization to 4-bit, caching gains for repeated prefixes) which bring the GPU need down substantially.

Asks / clarifications we need to finalize:
1) Can you confirm the peak minute RPS number (120) and whether the 120 is sustained across a 60s window or a sub-10s microburst?
2) Do you have an expected distribution of token lengths (e.g., % of requests under 128 tokens, 128–512, 512–1,000, >1,000)? That materially affects batching efficiency and KV cache benefits.
3) Any hard p95 latency SLO you must meet for production (e.g., 300ms, 500ms)? Our sizing trades off tokens/sec capacity for lower tail latency.

Next steps: once you confirm (1)–(3) we’ll lock a recommended slab configuration and a proposed commitment tier. I’ve looped Jordan (pricing) and Priyom (inference runtime / ops) to weigh in on tradeoffs — Priyom will propose an optimized configuration that reduces GPU count by applying burst pooling, prefix caching, and quantization recommendations.

Thanks,

Camila Reyes
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
camila_reyes@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123
Redwood Inference

Attachments: asterion_throughput_model.xlsx (spreadsheet), redwood-dedicated-proposal.pdf (proposal stub)

From: Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Camila — thanks, this is helpful and ambitious. A couple of clarifications from our side: 

- The 120 RPS number is the worst-case top-of-hour spike measured over a 10s window (so it’s a short burst, not sustained for the full minute). We have a broader background of ~18 RPS steady-state.
- Token distribution (rough approximate): ~55% under 256 tokens (short lookups), ~30% in 256–700, ~12% 700–1600 (long summaries), ~3% occasional >1600 when returning long patient history aggregations.
- Our latency SLO: p95 <= 450ms for text-only endpoints. For the long summarization flows we are willing to accept p95 <= 900ms if necessary (the user flow is async).

Also: we are open to hybrid strategies — a Dedicated slab for predictable baseline and a hosted burst pool for those 10s spikes. Curious how you modeled burst pools and warm-start vs. cold starts in the spreadsheet.

Sam
Senior Engineering Manager, AsterionBio
samuel.choi@asterionbio.com

-----Original message-----
On Fri, Apr 20, 2028 at 09:12 AM Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com> wrote:
> (see above)


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Samuel Choi <samuel.choi@asterionbio.com>, Emma Jones <emma.jones@asterionbio.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Apr 2028 11:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity proposal for AsterionBio — initial sizing and questions

Thanks for the details, Sam — that clarifies a lot. A few technical notes and an alternative sizing approach that will reduce the 1,400+ GPU topline estimate substantially: 

1) Burst vs sustained math: because the 120 RPS is a 10s microburst, you don’t need to commit 1,400 GPUs for the whole time window. Two levers help: a) a small dedicated slab sized for the sustained baseline (we’ll size for 18 RPS steady-state), b) a shared hosted burst pool (or a short-term elastic burst slab) that covers the 10s spikes.

2) Effective tokens/sec per GPU by request mix: the 130 tokens/sec baseline we shared assumes long 700-token responses are dominant. With your mix (55% <256), our internal microbench shows an average tokens/sec of ~210 tokens/sec per GPU when batching is efficient across mixed lengths (the shorter requests pack multiple per batch).

3) Recompute with pragmatic headroom:
- Peak tokens/sec instantaneous for 10s burst = 120 RPS * 700 tokens = 84,000 tokens/sec (same as Camila’s calc)
- Sustained baseline tokens/sec = 18 RPS * 420 avg = 7,560 tokens/sec
- Effective per-GPU tokens/sec (mixed profile, 70% util, 25% safety margin) = 210 * 0.70 * (1 - 0.25) ≈ 110 tokens/sec

Sizing recommendation (hybrid):
- Dedicated baseline slab: size to meet sustained baseline with room for steady growth: GPUs_baseline = ceil(7,560 / 110) = 69 GPUs
- Burst coverage (short 10s spikes): incremental GPUs needed = ceil((84,000 - (69 * 210 * 0.70)) / 110) ≈ ceil((84,000 - 10,143) / 110) ≈ 677 GPUs transient.

Operational notes:
- We can satisfy spike coverage by a mix of: (A) hosted burst pool (pay-as-you-go) + (B) short-term refundable burst slab (30–90 day) — both approaches reduce your upfront committed GPUs.
- Additional levers to lower total GPUs: prefix caching (for repeated clinical query patterns), 4-bit quantization (adds ~25–40% tokens/sec per GPU in our tests for this model class), and request-level batching tweaks.

I’ve updated the spreadsheet with scenario tabs for: baseline-only, hybrid-burst, and high-quant. If you want, I can run a stress test in our test cluster with a representative sample payload next week to validate the 210 tokens/sec estimate.

Priyom Das
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
priyom_das@redwood.com

Attachments: none

messages:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for taking the demo this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum toward a POC/PoV:

Summary of what BrightHealth told us on the call:
- Primary constraint: PHI-sensitive inference for triage and clinical summarization — must remain inside US with audit logs retained for 2 years.
- Target latency: p95 <= 200ms for single-turn generation (models that use a 2k token prompt).
- Current stack: mix of self-hosted Triton clusters (K8s + local NVMe cache) and some requests routed to another LLM API vendor (they didn’t name at first pass).
- Goal: reduce per-token unit cost while preserving deterministic tail-latency and KV cache reuse on long-running chat sessions.

Deployment options you asked us to clarify: Hosted API vs Dedicated reserved capacity vs Private VPC/on-prem. Below are our observations and a suggested sequencing for validation:

1) Hosted API (fastest path)
- Pros: fastest to stand up, we can demo with model pinning and per-route SLOs in ~48 hours; built-in prefix/KV caching and streaming enabled.
- Cons: needs VPC peering + encrypted transit for PHI; we can enable private endpoints but you’ll need an approved IP range and IP allowlist for the initial PoC.
- Fit: good if you can accept a private endpoint + platform-managed keys (we can also support customer-managed KMS for encryption-at-rest).

2) Dedicated (reserved GPU pools)
- Pros: predictable throughput, isolation, and capacity guarantees (monthly commit). Can attach to your VPC for egress controls.
- Cons: requires ~2–4 week lead time to reserve sizing and autoscaling policy tuning; pricing shifts to reserved-hour commitments.
- Fit: strong if you need consistent p95 latency with high QPS and want Redwood to manage runtime optimizations but keep network isolation.

3) Private VPC / On-prem (private control plane)
- Pros: full control of network, keys, and data residency. On-prem option can run in your data center (air-gapped) for audits.
- Cons: longest path (ops engagement + on-site infra validation), licensing & support contract required for private bundles.
- Fit: recommended for regulated production workloads that cannot rely on vendor-managed endpoints.

Migration notes (from your current vendors):
- From other LLM APIs: we can do a staged traffic cutover using model-variant routing + per-route fallbacks so you can compare outputs and tail latency.
- From self-hosted Triton clusters: we support importing model artifacts (if compatible) and offer quantization/compilation profiles that mirror inference behavior; for LLMs with custom kernels we’ll map differences and recommend a validation plan for output parity.

Immediate ask / next steps (proposed):
1) You confirm which two constraints we should prioritize in the PoC (e.g., p95 latency and PHI residency).
2) Marcus to run a quick sizing quiz with your infra lead (low-friction questions on peak QPS, avg seq length, KV cache hit rate, and allowed commit window).
3) Stephanie will send a one-page Mutual Action Plan (MAP) and a SOC2 evidence pointer for your security team to review.
4) If Hosted with private endpoints is acceptable, we can spin a PoC with synthetic traffic in 48–72 hours; if you need Dedicated or Private, schedule an ops scoping session next week.

I’ve attached a starter MAP and a snapshot of model comparison notes we discussed.

Thanks — speak soon.

Jordan Blake
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[jordan.blake@redwood.com] | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments: BrightHealth-MAP-v1.xlsx; model-comparison-snapshot.pdf

From: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: BrightHealth AI Platform Team <platform-team@brighthealth.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Jordan — thanks, this is helpful. Quick clarifications from our side:
- We need PHI never to leave our VPC for the production workload. For PoC we can evaluate Hosted private endpoints if traffic stays in-region and encryption keys are customer-managed (KMS).
- Our infra lead (Vikram) will share peak QPS numbers later today. We are currently at ~1200 requests/minute during daytime with avg sequence length 1.1k tokens. KV cache hit is about 35% for conversational workloads.
- Regarding the other API vendor: we use them for low-risk features today but want to evaluate Redwood for deterministic latency and lower unit costs. We will likely look for an incremental rollout (10% traffic -> 50% -> full) over 4–6 weeks.

Please move forward with the Hosted private endpoint PoC plan while we validate legal and infra constraints; also send the SOC2 pointer to security@brighthealth.ai.

Aisha
Head of Platform Engineering | BrightHealth AI

On Tue, Apr 11, 2028 at 10:15 AM Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com> wrote:
> (original message)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 12:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Aisha — thanks for the quick reply and the QPS / KV metrics. A few operational notes from the infra side to make the PoC smooth: 

1) For Hosted private endpoints + customer-managed KMS we need the following from your network team:
   - Allowed CIDR blocks for the private endpoint peering
   - A brief schedule for a short maintenance window (30–60 mins) during the PoC ramp to validate IP routing and firewall rules

2) Sizing note: given 1.1k avg token seq and 1200 req/min peak, initial PoC cluster we’d recommend a small reserved pool (4x A100-class GPUs equivalent) with autoscale headroom. That should give us the p95 headroom under typical load and let us test KV cache efficiency.

3) Migration path from Triton: we’ll produce a checklist for model artifact compatibility. If you can export model weights in a standard format (HF or ONNX) we’ll run a compatibility pass and return a quantization profile. For custom kernels we’ll propose a runtime shim for parity testing.

I’ll draft the infra checklist and share an ops call time for tomorrow.

—Marcus
Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: SOC2-Redwood-evidence-link.txt

From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 15:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Aisha — looping in Priya from our Customer Success org for onboarding timeline and contract placement.

Next small logistics items from our side:
- Attached MAP (BrightHealth-MAP-v1.xlsx) has milestones and acceptance criteria. Please confirm the two priority acceptance items we should instrument during PoC: (1) p95 latency with 2k prompt under synthetic load, and (2) end-to-end audit log delivery to your SIEM (we support both syslog and S3 sink).
- For your legal team: I’ve shared a short SOC2 evidence pointer (SOC2-Redwood-evidence-link.txt) and our standard PoC NDA link; if you prefer DocuSign we can send it to legal@brighthealth.ai.
- Pricing note: for the PoC we can offer a 30% credit on the first month of Dedicated reserved capacity should you elect to convert the PoC to a committed plan within 30 days — Marcus and I can get pricing lined up once Vikram confirms QPS.

If you confirm the two acceptance items above we’ll: (A) provision a Hosted private endpoint PoC with synthetic traffic in ~48 hours; (B) run a compatibility check on one exported model from your Triton cluster; (C) schedule a 30-minute ops window for peering verification.

Thanks — Stephanie
Director, Customer Success | Redwood Inference

Forwarded message header preserved for traceability.

3) Migration path from Triton: we’ll produce a checklist for model artifact compatibility. If you can export model weights in a standard format (HF or ONNX) we’ll run a compatibility pass and return a quantization profile. For custom kernels we’ll propose a runtime shim for parity testing.

I’ll draft the infra checklist and share an ops call time for tomorrow.

—Marcus
Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: SOC2-Redwood-evidence-link.txt


From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 15:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Aisha — looping in Priya from our Customer Success org for onboarding timeline and contract placement.

Next small logistics items from our side:
- Attached MAP (BrightHealth-MAP-v1.xlsx) has milestones and acceptance criteria. Please confirm the two priority acceptance items we should instrument during PoC: (1) p95 latency with 2k prompt under synthetic load, and (2) end-to-end audit log delivery to your SIEM (we support both syslog and S3 sink).
- For your legal team: I’ve shared a short SOC2 evidence pointer (SOC2-Redwood-evidence-link.txt) and our standard PoC NDA link; if you prefer DocuSign we can send it to legal@brighthealth.ai.
- Pricing note: for the PoC we can offer a 30% credit on the first month of Dedicated reserved capacity should you elect to convert the PoC to a committed plan within 30 days — Marcus and I can get pricing lined up once Vikram confirms QPS.

If you confirm the two acceptance items above we’ll: (A) provision a Hosted private endpoint PoC with synthetic traffic in ~48 hours; (B) run a compatibility check on one exported model from your Triton cluster; (C) schedule a 30-minute ops window for peering verification.

Thanks — Stephanie
Director, Customer Success | Redwood Inference

text:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for taking the demo this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum toward a POC/PoV:

Summary of what BrightHealth told us on the call:
- Primary constraint: PHI-sensitive inference for triage and clinical summarization — must remain inside US with audit logs retained for 2 years.
- Target latency: p95 <= 200ms for single-turn generation (models that use a 2k token prompt).
- Current stack: mix of self-hosted Triton clusters (K8s + local NVMe cache) and some requests routed to another LLM API vendor (they didn’t name at first pass).
- Goal: reduce per-token unit cost while preserving deterministic tail-latency and KV cache reuse on long-running chat sessions.

Deployment options you asked us to clarify: Hosted API vs Dedicated reserved capacity vs Private VPC/on-prem. Below are our observations and a suggested sequencing for validation:

1) Hosted API (fastest path)
- Pros: fastest to stand up, we can demo with model pinning and per-route SLOs in ~48 hours; built-in prefix/KV caching and streaming enabled.
- Cons: needs VPC peering + encrypted transit for PHI; we can enable private endpoints but you’ll need an approved IP range and IP allowlist for the initial PoC.
- Fit: good if you can accept a private endpoint + platform-managed keys (we can also support customer-managed KMS for encryption-at-rest).

2) Dedicated (reserved GPU pools)
- Pros: predictable throughput, isolation, and capacity guarantees (monthly commit). Can attach to your VPC for egress controls.
- Cons: requires ~2–4 week lead time to reserve sizing and autoscaling policy tuning; pricing shifts to reserved-hour commitments.
- Fit: strong if you need consistent p95 latency with high QPS and want Redwood to manage runtime optimizations but keep network isolation.

3) Private VPC / On-prem (private control plane)
- Pros: full control of network, keys, and data residency. On-prem option can run in your data center (air-gapped) for audits.
- Cons: longest path (ops engagement + on-site infra validation), licensing & support contract required for private bundles.
- Fit: recommended for regulated production workloads that cannot rely on vendor-managed endpoints.

Migration notes (from your current vendors):
- From other LLM APIs: we can do a staged traffic cutover using model-variant routing + per-route fallbacks so you can compare outputs and tail latency.
- From self-hosted Triton clusters: we support importing model artifacts (if compatible) and offer quantization/compilation profiles that mirror inference behavior; for LLMs with custom kernels we’ll map differences and recommend a validation plan for output parity.

Immediate ask / next steps (proposed):
1) You confirm which two constraints we should prioritize in the PoC (e.g., p95 latency and PHI residency).
2) Marcus to run a quick sizing quiz with your infra lead (low-friction questions on peak QPS, avg seq length, KV cache hit rate, and allowed commit window).
3) Stephanie will send a one-page Mutual Action Plan (MAP) and a SOC2 evidence pointer for your security team to review.
4) If Hosted with private endpoints is acceptable, we can spin a PoC with synthetic traffic in 48–72 hours; if you need Dedicated or Private, schedule an ops scoping session next week.

I’ve attached a starter MAP and a snapshot of model comparison notes we discussed.

Thanks — speak soon.

Jordan Blake
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[jordan.blake@redwood.com] | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments: BrightHealth-MAP-v1.xlsx; model-comparison-snapshot.pdf


From: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: BrightHealth AI Platform Team <platform-team@brighthealth.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Jordan — thanks, this is helpful. Quick clarifications from our side:
- We need PHI never to leave our VPC for the production workload. For PoC we can evaluate Hosted private endpoints if traffic stays in-region and encryption keys are customer-managed (KMS).
- Our infra lead (Vikram) will share peak QPS numbers later today. We are currently at ~1200 requests/minute during daytime with avg sequence length 1.1k tokens. KV cache hit is about 35% for conversational workloads.
- Regarding the other API vendor: we use them for low-risk features today but want to evaluate Redwood for deterministic latency and lower unit costs. We will likely look for an incremental rollout (10% traffic -> 50% -> full) over 4–6 weeks.

Please move forward with the Hosted private endpoint PoC plan while we validate legal and infra constraints; also send the SOC2 pointer to security@brighthealth.ai.

Aisha
Head of Platform Engineering | BrightHealth AI

From: Alex Moreno <alex@stagelabs.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Sage Events <events@stagelabs.io>
Date: Tue, 20 Mar 2028 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Onsite demo at Redwood HQ — logistics and NDA

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the quick demo last week — team loved the throughput numbers. The execs at StageLabs would like to come onsite to Redwood HQ for a focused sandbox/ops day so our platform and infra teams can validate the integration for a summer pilot.

Rough ask: one-day visit for 6 people the week of Mar 27 (flexible on day), a 90–120 minute deep-dive with your infra + security folks, and a short run-of-show (30m) with the product team. Can you confirm:
- Available rooms for a mid-size workshop (6–8 people) with projector and network access
- Visitor check-in process (badges / ID) and whether we need to pre-sign any NDAs / forms
- Parking passes or nearby visitor garages

I can share names and travel ETA once we lock a date. If there's a preferred legal contact or NDA link you want us to use, send it over and we'll start the paperwork.

Attaching a tentative attendee list (names obf.) so you can see roles.

Best,
Alex
-----
Alex Moreno
Head of Partnerships, StageLabs
alex@stagelabs.io | +1 415-555-0198

Attachment: StageLabs_visitor_list.csv (stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Alex Moreno <alex@stagelabs.io>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 21 Mar 2028 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Onsite demo at Redwood HQ — logistics and NDA

Hi Alex — great to hear, we'd love to host StageLabs. Proposed logistics below; pick whatever works best for your team and we'll lock it in.

Options (week of Mar 27):
- Wed, Mar 29 — 10:30–12:30 (preferred)
- Thu, Mar 30 — 14:00–16:00

We can reserve Maple 3 (small workshop room) + overflow desks in Suite A if you expect breakout work. Amenities: projector, wired network line, and a sandbox VM cluster (request 24h in advance).

Check-in & compliance:
- We require a signed visitor NDA / access form for contractors and partner engineers. I created a DocuSign packet for StageLabs — link below. Please have attendees sign before arrival to speed check-in.
- Day-of flow: visitor kiosk at Lobby B — badges printed on arrival, then escorted to Maple 3. If any attendee needs to use private lab machines, we'll arrange a controlled access session and ask for laptop MACs in advance.

Parking: short-term visitor parking available in Lot C (reserve up to 3 passes). If you need more, there is the Novato Parking Garage 2 blocks away (free first 2 hours with validation).

Next steps:
1) Tell us which date/time you prefer.
2) Complete DocuSign packet: https://app.docusign.com/prepare/ABC-stage-non-disclosure
3) Send final attendee names + arrival ETA (we'll produce badges).

I've attached our HQ badge form for attendees to pre-fill if you prefer. Kimberly will own escorting and security intake; Jordan will handle AV and room setup.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0112

Attachments: Redwood_HQ_badge_form.pdf (stub); internal_map_maple3.png (ops only - not attached here)

- Luis Vega (Security) — ETA 10:25
- Alex Moreno (Partnerships) — ETA 10:00

Parking request: can we reserve 3 visitor passes in Lot C? We'll try to compact cars but may need the validation at Garage B in case Lot C fills.

—Alex

source.subject: Re: Amendment v2 - Capacity swap, billing tranche & MSA footnote alignment
messages:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
To: Olivia Turner <olivia@brightlane.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 1 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Amendment v2 - Capacity swap, billing tranche & MSA footnote alignment

Olivia,

Thanks again for the conversation earlier — attaching the draft amendment we discussed (capacity swap between SKU-N and SKU-P, plus a one-off billing tranche to smooth the phasing). Key asks on our side:

- Confirm the net capacity after swap (we've proposed moving 1200 GPU-hours from SKU-N into SKU-P for March–May).
- Accept the billing tranche language so invoicing is split 60/40 for the implementation month.
- Minor MSA footnote to preserve rollback protection in case of provisioning issues.

I attached Amendment_v2_draft.docx for your legal to redline. If your team prefers, we can propose exact language for the MSA footnote and billing clause.

Proposed next step: your legal sends redlines and a short list of open points; we sync end of day tomorrow for a final readout. Happy to hop on a quick call if that speeds things.

Thanks,
Connor O'Brien
Account Exec – Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0198

Attachment: Redwood_Amendment_v2_draft.docx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document)
From: Olivia Turner <olivia@brightlane.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Cc: BrightLane Legal <legal@brightlane.com>, Matthew Chu <matthew@brightlane.com>
Date: Thu, 1 Mar 2028 11:40:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Amendment v2 - Capacity swap, billing tranche & MSA footnote alignment

Connor —

Thanks for the draft. Our counsel reviewed and attached a redlined version (BrightLane_Amendment_v2_redlines.docx) and a one-page summary of material changes (redline_summary_brightlane.pdf). High-level items they flagged:

1) Billing tranche wording: they want the tranche to be described as a "one-time transitional invoice adjustment" (rather than percent split) and request an additional sentence clarifying that it does not affect future rate tiers.

2) Capacity swap mechanics: they asked for explicit acceptance criteria on the re-assigned nodes (uptime SLA and integration cutover window).

3) MSA footnote: they prefer not to introduce a rollback protection mechanism that would automatically trigger capacity return; instead, they asked for a manual remediation path with a 14-day notice to Redwood.

They've flagged a couple of smaller editorial/definitions issues and one data residency sentence that we need to confirm.

On timing: legal can turn around a counter-redline by Friday but asked for Redwood to propose precise fallback language for the MSA footnote (so they can accept or propose edits).

Olivia
Head of Partnerships
BrightLane

-----Original Message-----
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Sent: Thu, 1 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
To: Olivia Turner <olivia@brightlane.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Subject: Amendment v2 - Capacity swap, billing tranche & MSA footnote alignment

[attached: Redwood_Amendment_v2_draft.docx]
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 2 Mar 2028 08:05:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: BrightLane redlines — need legal + commercial take

Team — forwarding BrightLane's redlines (attached). Quick summary: they accepted the capacity swap concept but want:

• different billing phrasing ("transitional invoice adjustment")
• formal acceptance criteria for the reassigned nodes (uptime + integration cutover window)
• no automatic rollback; a manual remediation route with 14-day notice

Attachments: BrightLane_Amendment_v2_redlines.docx, redline_summary_brightlane.pdf

Ask: could legal (Karthik) confirm whether the manual remediation route is acceptable from our risk side, and commercial (Laura) whether we can live with the billing phrasing change without eroding the revenue recognition model. If we need to push back, list the minimum language we must keep (e.g., rollback protection language and a carve-out for emergency provisioning).

If possible, can we have a one-pager counter proposal by EOD tomorrow? I can set a 30-min sync with BrightLane for Monday morning once we have a position.

Thanks — Connor

-----Forwarded message attached-----
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 3 Mar 2028 16:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Fwd: BrightLane redlines — need legal + commercial take

Connor —

Thanks for the flag and for summarizing. Read through BrightLane's redline and here's our recommended path (summary + proposed language snippets attached as redwood_counter_amendment_v2.docx):

1) Billing tranche: we can accept the phrase "one-time transitional invoice adjustment" provided we add a parenthetical sentence: "For clarity, this adjustment is a one-time allocation and will not be treated as a change to rate tiers, rate cards, or future per-token/per-embedding pricing." This preserves revenue recognition while giving them the wording they requested.

2) Capacity acceptance criteria: add an appendix (Exhibit H — Acceptance Criteria) with a short checklist: provisioning deadline, 99.5% uptime SLA for the first 30 days post-cutover, and a two-day integration window for ops to validate KV-cache persistence. This is reasonable and helps avoid scope creep.

3) Rollback / remediation: we should avoid language that allows unilateral rollback. Counter-proposal: keep "rollback protection" but gate it behind a remediation workflow: "In the event of systemic provisioning failure materially affecting production, Customer or Redwood will issue notice; Redwood will, within 14 days, propose remedial steps including potential capacity re-assignment. Any re-assignment becomes effective only upon mutual written agreement or a defined arbitration path." This addresses their ask while preserving our operational controls.

4) Data residency sentence: confirm that the reassigned nodes remain in the same region (US-West) unless otherwise agreed; add explicit sentence to the amendment.

Next steps:
- Laura: if commercial is good with the parenthetical to preserve billing recognition, we'll send the counter-redline to BrightLane tomorrow morning and propose Mon 09:00 PT for a 30-min sync.
- Connor: I'll attach the redline with tracked changes and the one-pager summary. Send to Olivia and copy legal.

Karthik
Senior Counsel, Commercial Contracts
Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood_counter_amendment_v2.docx (tracked changes)

source.subject: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech
messages:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Nguyen <alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com>, Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@sandbartech.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 28 Feb 2028 14:12:00 -0800
Subject: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech

Hi Alex / Elena —

Really appreciate you both taking the time today. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we keep momentum:

Highlights from the demo
- Retrieval and ranking flow matched your sample prompts; relevance looked strong against the test set you shared.
- End-to-end latency in the demo environment averaged ~180ms for single-turn QA (demo config).
- Built-in caching eliminated repeated token costs for exercise queries.

Proposed trial (6 weeks) — primary goals
1) Validate retrieval relevance at scale (50k queries over 6 weeks) with >80% pass rate on your acceptance prompt set.
2) Meet application latency target (we recommended 200ms p50; if you prefer we can relax to 300ms — see notes below).
3) Verify integration and security: SSO, allowlist, and audit-log delivery to your SIEM.

Evaluation plan & success criteria (high level)
- Acceptance prompt set: you provide 200 gold prompts + pass/fail rubric. Pass = top-3 contains correct answer per rubric. Target: 80%+ pass.
- Latency: p50/p95 targets for the production-like workload. Default suggestion: p50 < 200ms, p95 < 600ms.
- Cost baseline: we'll track token/unit cost during the trial and report unit economics per route.

Timeline (proposed)
Week 0 (kickoff): finalize scope, owners, and sign trial addendum
Week 1: connect SSO & allowlist; baseline ingestion of sample corpus
Weeks 2-5: ramp traffic for evaluation; weekly checkpoints with metrics
Week 6: final report + next-step recommendations (Dedicated/private sizing or hosted production rollout)

Owners
- Redwood: Ben Carter (sales lead), Marissa Cole (CS/eng owner), Jordan Blake (solutions/infra)
- Sandbar: Alex Nguyen (product), Elena Ruiz (IT/security) — please confirm any additional stakeholders (procurement, legal).

Next steps (asks for this week)
1) Confirm acceptance prompt set owner and target date for delivery.
2) Confirm whether latency target should stay at 200ms p50 or be relaxed to 300ms p50.
3) Sign the trial addendum (I attached a draft) or confirm procurement path.
4) Propose 60-90 minute kickoff slot week of Mar 12.

Attachments: proposed-trial-charter-v1.pdf (PDF, charter + metrics)
Recording: Sandbar-demo-recording.mp4 (30m) — link also in Drive: https://drive.redwood.com/s/abc123-trial-charter

Happy to run a quick technical deep-dive if Jordan or Marissa should join separately.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0142
From: Alex Nguyen <alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@sandbartech.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 01 Mar 2028 09:18:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech

Thanks Ben — great demo yesterday. A few clarifying items from our side before we confirm kickoff:  

1) Latency: our product team is more comfortable with p50 < 300ms given our enrichment pipeline. Can Redwood operate at 300ms p50 while keeping relevance within the target? If there are trade-offs we should be explicit about them.

2) Data movement: we want to know what exact artifacts you need to ingest and any transformation steps. Will you accept a nightly S3 drop of the corpus?

3) Network / security: please send IP ranges and any firewall rules we need to allow. Our security team will also want to review your SOC 2 report.

4) Procurement: we can move quickly but need a sample SOW and confirmation whether a pilot PO can be used to start (we prefer a smaller pilot PO rather than a full MSA at kickoff).

If you can answer the above we can align procurement and schedule the kickoff.

— Alex
Alex Nguyen
Product Lead, Search
Sandbar Tech
alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Nguyen <alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 01 Mar 2028 11:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech

On Wed, Mar 1, 2028 at 9:18 AM Alex Nguyen <alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com> wrote:
> Thanks Ben — great demo yesterday. A few clarifying items from our side before we confirm kickoff:  
> 
> 1) Latency: our product team is more comfortable with p50 < 300ms given our enrichment pipeline. Can Redwood operate at 300ms p50 while keeping relevance within the target? If there are trade-offs we should be explicit about them.
> 
> 2) Data movement: we want to know what exact artifacts you need to ingest and any transformation steps. Will you accept a nightly S3 drop of the corpus?
> 
> 3) Network / security: please send IP ranges and any firewall rules we need to allow. Our security team will also want to review your SOC 2 report.
> 
> 4) Procurement: we can move quickly but need a sample SOW and confirmation whether a pilot PO can be used to start (we prefer a smaller pilot PO rather than a full MSA at kickoff).

Thanks Alex — answers inline below: 

1) Latency: Yes — we can run the trial with p50 target of 300ms. Note: relaxing p50 increases the likelihood we use denser batching or larger effective batch windows to hit cost targets; relevance impact is typically negligible for retrieval-first flows but we’ll monitor drift. We’ll include both the 200ms and 300ms baselines in the weekly metric report so you can pick.

2) Data movement: nightly S3 drop is perfect. We need a manifest CSV and a sample document mapping (id, title, body, metadata fields). Jordan, can you own the detailed schema + ingestion checklist?

3) Network/security: Jordan will provide allowlist IPs and the minimal ports we require (443 outbound + health-check endpoints). Also: SOC 2 report and security questionnaire are attached via the Drive link.

4) Procurement: pilot PO is fine. We typically have a short trial addendum + pilot SOW (I attached an updated charter v1.1 with a procurement appendix). If Procurement needs a sample SOW, Marissa can share our standard pilot SOW template.

Attachments: proposed-trial-charter-v1.1.pdf (updated)
Drive: https://drive.redwood.com/s/abc123-trial-charter

Marissa / Jordan — can you confirm (a) availability for kickoff week of Mar 12 and (b) who from Sandbar procurement/legal we should loop?

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0142

--
Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai> will own network allowlist details and ingestion checklist.
Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai> will own SOW / pilot procurement attachments.
From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@sandbartech.com>

Date: Tue, 28 Feb 2028 14:12:00 -0800
Subject: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech

Hi Alex / Elena —

Really appreciate you both taking the time today. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we keep momentum:

Highlights from the demo
- Retrieval and ranking flow matched your sample prompts; relevance looked strong against the test set you shared.
- End-to-end latency in the demo environment averaged ~180ms for single-turn QA (demo config).
- Built-in caching eliminated repeated token costs for exercise queries.

Proposed trial (6 weeks) — primary goals
1) Validate retrieval relevance at scale (50k queries over 6 weeks) with >80% pass rate on your acceptance prompt set.
2) Meet application latency target (we recommended 200ms p50; if you prefer we can relax to 300ms — see notes below).
3) Verify integration and security: SSO, allowlist, and audit-log delivery to your SIEM.

Evaluation plan & success criteria (high level)
- Acceptance prompt set: you provide 200 gold prompts + pass/fail rubric. Pass = top-3 contains correct answer per rubric. Target: 80%+ pass.
- Latency: p50/p95 targets for the production-like workload. Default suggestion: p50 < 200ms, p95 < 600ms.
- Cost baseline: we'll track token/unit cost during the trial and report unit economics per route.

Timeline (proposed)
Week 0 (kickoff): finalize scope, owners, and sign trial addendum
Week 1: connect SSO & allowlist; baseline ingestion of sample corpus
Weeks 2-5: ramp traffic for evaluation; weekly checkpoints with metrics
Week 6: final report + next-step recommendations (Dedicated/private sizing or hosted production rollout)

Owners
- Redwood: Ben Carter (sales lead), Marissa Cole (CS/eng owner), Jordan Blake (solutions/infra)
- Sandbar: Alex Nguyen (product), Elena Ruiz (IT/security) — please confirm any additional stakeholders (procurement, legal).

Next steps (asks for this week)
1) Confirm acceptance prompt set owner and target date for delivery.
2) Confirm whether latency target should stay at 200ms p50 or be relaxed to 300ms p50.
3) Sign the trial addendum (I attached a draft) or confirm procurement path.

source.subject: AcmePay onboarding: orchestration plan for SDK cutover
messages:
From: Evan Liu <evan.liu@acmepay.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
Cc: Integrify Onboarding <implementation@acmepay.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Feb 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: AcmePay onboarding kickoff

Hi Chloe —

Thanks for the product demo last week. We attached the MVP integration spec and a mutual action plan so we can align on the cutover window and responsibilities. High level asks from our side:
- SDK install and a short sample (Node and Go) for our backend team
- Clear rollback plan for endpoint migration (sandbox -> prod)
- Definitive guidance for retries/timeouts and 429 handling — we have aggressive SLAs and need to avoid double-billing for tokens during retries

Can your implementation lead propose a technical checklist and a 2-week runway? We’re targeting a late-February soft-launch.

Best,
Evan
Evan Liu
Head of Integrations, AcmePay

Attachments: acme-mvp-integration-specs.pdf, acme-mutual-action-plan.xlsx

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Evan Liu <evan.liu@acmepay.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Feb 2028 11:20:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: AcmePay onboarding kickoff

Evan —

Appreciate the docs. We can definitely meet the late-February soft-launch if we follow a compact cutover plan. I’ve attached a small SDK bundle with samples and a one-page runbook. High level plan (draft):
1) Sandbox integration + sample validation (this week)
2) Mutual-action cutover prep (test traffic, observability checks)
3) Production DNS/endpoint switch (scheduled 2-hour window)
4) 1x rollback point + fallback model variant if token limits spike

Quick SDK guidance (included in sample-sdk-snippets.zip):
- Node example (axios):
  const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.RW_KEY, timeoutMs: 12000, maxRetries: 3 });
  // simple retry backoff: exponential with jitter (base 250ms)

- Go example (http.Client):
  client.Timeout = 12 * time.Second
  // retry: 3 attempts, exponential backoff + full jitter

Rate-limit policy we recommend: treat 429 as transient. Implement exponential backoff with full jitter and cap retries to 5 for idempotent calls. For non-idempotent generation requests, prefer client-side token budgeting and a single retry that does a quick status check with the server-side request id to avoid duplicate token spend.

I’ll loop in our Implementation PM and dev lead: Vivek (program owner) and Jordan (runtime/SDK). Kimberly from CSM will coordinate cutover comms to AcmePay. Proposed kickoff: Mon Feb 13 10am PT — ok for you?

Thanks,
Chloe Martin
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference

Attachments: sample-sdk-snippets.zip

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 24 Feb 2028 16:42:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: AcmePay onboarding kickoff — internal checklist

Chloe / team — quick status update and confirmation for cutover window on Mon Feb 28 02:00–04:00 PT (2-hour maintenance window). Progress:
- Node + Go samples validated against Acme staging; added X-Request-ID and idempotency checks.
- SDK: default retry policy updated (idempotent endpoints 5 retries with exp backoff + full jitter; generation endpoints 1 retry with server request-id de-dup).
- Rate-limiting: client-side concurrency cap default set to 8; added helper to switch to token-batching (50ms window) in high-throughput paths.
- Observability: added dashboard tile and alert for token-consumption spike and 5xx threshold.

Attachments: cutover-ops-playbook.md (pushed to shared Drive)

Kimberly — can you confirm external comms (notification + rollback steps) scheduled for Fri Feb 25 so Acme has 72h notice? Evan — please confirm the maintenance window. If all good, we’ll proceed with the planned switch and monitor the three cutover signals closely.

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Senior Software Engineer, Redwood Inference

> On Feb 11, 2028, Chloe Martin wrote:
> > Team — forwarding Acme’s spec. Key asks and proposed owners below...

Evan —

1) Sandbox integration + sample validation (this week)
2) Mutual-action cutover prep (test traffic, observability checks)
3) Production DNS/endpoint switch (scheduled 2-hour window)
4) 1x rollback point + fallback model variant if token limits spike

Quick SDK guidance (included in sample-sdk-snippets.zip):
- Node example (axios):
  const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.RW_KEY, timeoutMs: 12000, maxRetries: 3 });

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Feb 2028 08:05:00 -08:00
Subject: Fwd: AcmePay onboarding kickoff — internal checklist

Team — forwarding Acme’s spec. Key asks and proposed owners below. Please reply with any blockers.

Owners & immediate tasks:
- Vivek (Impl PM): finalize mutual-action plan milestones, confirm cutover window, own rollback decision
- Jordan (Runtime/SDK): validate Node/Go samples with Acme devs, ensure retry semantics and idempotency tokens for between-client retries
- Kimberly (CSM): schedule cutover comms and DocuSign for updated SLA addendum if needed

Technical checklist for Jordan (priority):
- Confirm sandbox keys and prod key rotation flow
- Add request-id header on all generation calls (X-Request-ID) so we can tie retries to server outcomes- Implement anti-double-spend: server returns canonical operation id; client verifies before re-sending a generation call after a 429
- Timeout settings: connect 2s, read 8–12s; overall request timeout 12s suggested
- Retries: idempotent endpoints: up to 5 retries (exp backoff + full jitter), generation endpoints (non-idempotent): 1 retry but use request-id check
- Rate limiting: enforce client-side concurrency cap default 8 concurrent requests per process; recommend token batching for small prompt bursts (batch window 50ms) to reduce per-token cost

Sample snippet (pseudocode to include in SDK examples):
---
async function callGenerate(payload) {
  const reqId = uuid();
  let attempt = 0;
  while (attempt <= 3) {
    try {
      const res = await client.post('/v1/generate', payload, { headers: { 'X-Request-ID': reqId }, timeout: 12000 });
      return res.data;
    } catch (err) {
      if (err.status === 429) {
        // full jitter backoff
        const sleep = Math.random() * (Math.pow(2, attempt) * 250);
        await sleepMs(sleep);
        attempt++;
        continue;
      }
      throw err;
    }
  }
  throw new Error('Retries exhausted');
}
---

Observability & cutover signals (so we can abort quickly):
- 15m rolling token consumption spike > 2x baseline
- 5xx rate > 1% over 10m
- Increase in avg latency > 50% -> trigger fallback to smaller model variant

Jordan — if you can validate samples against their staging by Feb 15 we can keep the Feb 24 soft-launch.

Chloe

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 24 Feb 2028 16:42:00 -08:00

- Rate-limiting: client-side concurrency cap default set to 8; added helper to switch to token-batching (50ms window) in high-throughput paths.
- Observability: added dashboard tile and alert for token-consumption spike and 5xx threshold.

Subject: Re: AcmePay onboarding kickoff

Hi Chloe —

Thanks for the product demo last week. We attached the MVP integration spec and a mutual action plan so we can align on the cutover window and responsibilities. High level asks from our side:
- SDK install and a short sample (Node and Go) for our backend team
- Clear rollback plan for endpoint migration (sandbox -> prod)
- Definitive guidance for retries/timeouts and 429 handling — we have aggressive SLAs and need to avoid double-billing for tokens during retries

Can your implementation lead propose a technical checklist and a 2-week runway? We’re targeting a late-February soft-launch.

Best,
Evan
Evan Liu
Head of Integrations, AcmePay

Attachments: acme-mvp-integration-specs.pdf, acme-mutual-action-plan.xlsx

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Evan Liu <evan.liu@acmepay.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Feb 2028 11:20:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: AcmePay onboarding kickoff

Evan —

Appreciate the docs. We can definitely meet the late-February soft-launch if we follow a compact cutover plan. I’ve attached a small SDK bundle with samples and a one-page runbook. High level plan (draft):
1) Sandbox integration + sample validation (this week)
2) Mutual-action cutover prep (test traffic, observability checks)
3) Production DNS/endpoint switch (scheduled 2-hour window)
4) 1x rollback point + fallback model variant if token limits spike

Quick SDK guidance (included in sample-sdk-snippets.zip):
- Node example (axios):
  const client = new RedwoodClient({ apiKey: process.env.RW_KEY, timeoutMs: 12000, maxRetries: 3 });
  // simple retry backoff: exponential with jitter (base 250ms)

- Go example (http.Client):
  client.Timeout = 12 * time.Second
  // retry: 3 attempts, exponential backoff + full jitter

Rate-limit policy we recommend: treat 429 as transient. Implement exponential backoff with full jitter and cap retries to 5 for idempotent calls. For non-idempotent generation requests, prefer client-side token budgeting and a single retry that does a quick status check with the server-side request id to avoid duplicate token spend.

I’ll loop in our Implementation PM and dev lead: Vivek (program owner) and Jordan (runtime/SDK). Kimberly from CSM will coordinate cutover comms to AcmePay. Proposed kickoff: Mon Feb 13 10am PT — ok for you?

Thanks,
Chloe Martin
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference

Attachments: sample-sdk-snippets.zip

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Feb 2028 08:05:00 -08:00
Subject: Fwd: AcmePay onboarding kickoff — internal checklist

Team — forwarding Acme’s spec. Key asks and proposed owners below. Please reply with any blockers.

Owners & immediate tasks:
- Vivek (Impl PM): finalize mutual-action plan milestones, confirm cutover window, own rollback decision
- Jordan (Runtime/SDK): validate Node/Go samples with Acme devs, ensure retry semantics and idempotency tokens for between-client retries
- Kimberly (CSM): schedule cutover comms and DocuSign for updated SLA addendum if needed

Technical checklist for Jordan (priority):
- Confirm sandbox keys and prod key rotation flow
- Add request-id header on all generation calls (X-Request-ID) so we can tie retries to server outcomes- Implement anti-double-spend: server returns canonical operation id; client verifies before re-sending a generation call after a 429
- Timeout settings: connect 2s, read 8–12s; overall request timeout 12s suggested
- Retries: idempotent endpoints: up to 5 retries (exp backoff + full jitter), generation endpoints (non-idempotent): 1 retry but use request-id check
- Rate limiting: enforce client-side concurrency cap default 8 concurrent requests per process; recommend token batching for small prompt bursts (batch window 50ms) to reduce per-token cost

Sample snippet (pseudocode to include in SDK examples):
---
async function callGenerate(payload) {
  const reqId = uuid();
  let attempt = 0;
  while (attempt <= 3) {
    try {
      const res = await client.post('/v1/generate', payload, { headers: { 'X-Request-ID': reqId }, timeout: 12000 });
      return res.data;
    } catch (err) {
      if (err.status === 429) {
        // full jitter backoff
        const sleep = Math.random() * (Math.pow(2, attempt) * 250);
        await sleepMs(sleep);
        attempt++;
        continue;
      }
      throw err;
    }
  }
  throw new Error('Retries exhausted');
}
---

Observability & cutover signals (so we can abort quickly):
- 15m rolling token consumption spike > 2x baseline
- 5xx rate > 1% over 10m
- Increase in avg latency > 50% -> trigger fallback to smaller model variant

Jordan — if you can validate samples against their staging by Feb 15 we can keep the Feb 24 soft-launch.

Chloe

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 24 Feb 2028 16:42:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: AcmePay onboarding kickoff — internal checklist

Chloe / team — quick status update and confirmation for cutover window on Mon Feb 28 02:00–04:00 PT (2-hour maintenance window). Progress:
- Node + Go samples validated against Acme staging; added X-Request-ID and idempotency checks.
- SDK: default retry policy updated (idempotent endpoints 5 retries with exp backoff + full jitter; generation endpoints 1 retry with server request-id de-dup).
- Rate-limiting: client-side concurrency cap default set to 8; added helper to switch to token-batching (50ms window) in high-throughput paths.
- Observability: added dashboard tile and alert for token-consumption spike and 5xx threshold.

Attachments: cutover-ops-playbook.md (pushed to shared Drive)

Kimberly — can you confirm external comms (notification + rollback steps) scheduled for Fri Feb 25 so Acme has 72h notice? Evan — please confirm the maintenance window. If all good, we’ll proceed with the planned switch and monitor the three cutover signals closely.

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Senior Software Engineer, Redwood Inference

messages:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Thanks for taking the time today — quick recap from our discovery so we can size and propose the right pilot. Please see bullets and the specific asks at the bottom.

What we heard on the call:
- Primary product: real-time order recommendations surfaced during checkout.
- 60% of requests are streaming responses (progressive recommendations) vs 40% full-response.
- Target e2e SLO: 95th percentile start-of-stream < 300ms, full-response 95th < 700ms for median payloads.
- Typical request: 12 tokens prompt, ~70 tokens average response, but peak flows can have 200+ tokens/resp.
- Traffic pattern: steady baseline during day with short bursts (15–45s) during promotions. Peak windows observed ~10x steady QPS.

Key unknowns we need to size correctly (please share if available):
1) 48–72h sample of request logs (timestamp, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, streaming flag) OR a CSV export (see example attached).
2) Peak QPS and steady-state QPS by hour/day (if you have 95th/99th percentiles for windows that helps).
3) Typical concurrency per user/session, and throttling behavior in-app (max concurrent open streams).
4) Any strict bandwidth/region constraints (we heard US-only but confirm).

Our initial hypotheses (to validate):
- Steady-state: ~150–300 QPS (depending on region split).
- Burst peak: ~1,200–2,000 QPS for 30–90s during promotions.
- Avg tokens/sec baseline: nominal; token-based batching will meaningfully change cost at peak.

Next steps / asks:
- Emily: share the 48–72h CSV sample and any recent peak windows (attach to HubSpot / Drive).
- Jordan + Marcus: we’ll run a quick sizing sweep once we have the sample and return a short proposal (hosted vs dedicated recommendation with rough cost & attendees for pilot).
- Goal: draft a pilot plan and SLA note by Thu (48 hours) so NimbleCart can review with infra/procurement.

Attachments: sample CSV template attached (nimble_example_traffic.csv).

Thanks — Ava
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com

---
(Attachment: nimble_example_traffic.csv — CSV stub, expected columns: timestamp, request_id, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, streaming_bool, region)

From: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: NimbleCart Infra <infra@nimblecart.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 17:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Thanks Ava — great recap. Attaching a 72h export and a short SLA doc for context (nimble_sla_requirements.pdf). High level answers inline: 

- Steady-state QPS (US region): ~220 QPS (weekday daytime).
- Observed promotional peaks: 1,800–2,100 QPS for ~30–60s windows; happens ~3–5x/day during promotions.
- Token distribution: median response ~68 tokens, 75th ~120 tokens, occasional long tails to 350 tokens. Prompt tokens mostly 8–20.
- Streaming: ~60% of calls are streaming; client opens up to 6 concurrent streams per user during heavy checkout sessions.
- Regional constraint: US primary, EU negligible for now.

We attached nimble_example_traffic.csv (48h sample: includes a 45s peak window). Let us know if the columns/format don’t work. We also want an initial cost ballpark and whether you think Hosted API will meet the SLOs or if we should plan Dedicated.

Emily Zhang
Head of Infra, Product
NimbleCart
emily@nimblecart.com

(Attachment: nimble_example_traffic.csv — 48h sample, includes a 45s peak window at 2028-02-19 14:22:xx UTC)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 22 Feb 2028 08:09:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Appreciate the data, Emily — dug into the CSV quickly and here are initial sizing notes (high-level, conservative):

Observations from sample:
- Steady: ~210 QPS, median response ~68 tokens, streaming fraction ~58%.
- Peak window (45s): sustained ~1,950 QPS mapped to many short-lived sessions.

Initial sizing guidance (to budget/propose pilot):
- Hosted (fast time-to-value): can likely meet the SLOs for steady-state with appropriate batching and streaming optimizations. For peaks, Hosted with auto-burst may work but risk of throttling depending on other tenants — recommend a committed Dedicated lane for predictable peak handling.
- Suggested pilot footprint: 2 x dedicated GPU nodes for steady-state (handles ~250 QPS comfortably with batching + cache). Add burst pool sizing: 6–8 nodes or equivalent autoscale to handle 1,800–2,000 QPS with target 95th start-of-stream < 300ms. Exact node type TBD based on quantization profile and kernel mix.
- Token throughput: at 70 tokens/resp, peak token/sec at 2,000 QPS ≈ 140k tokens/sec — this drives batching and memory/KV cache needs.
- Concurrency: client opens up to 6 streams/user — we need to model concurrent KV cache pressure and connection limits.

Short asks from our side to finalize numbers:
1) Can you confirm any hard SLA on cost vs latency for promotions? (procurement input).
2) Provide a 1-hour high-resolution log around a representative peak (preferably raw request-level timestamps) so we can run a burst simulation.

Jordan will draft a 2-page pilot proposal (hosted vs dedicated tradeoff, estimated cost range, success criteria) and share by EOD. Ava / Marcus will pick the proposal owners.

-Jordan
Jordan Blake
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

To: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 22 Feb 2028 08:37:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Thanks Jordan — that aligns with internal modeling. We can provide the 1-hour high-res log around the biggest promo tomorrow morning (UTC).

Procurement note: we do have a flexible budget for promotional capacity but need an estimated monthly delta (ballpark) between hosted and a small Dedicated commit for initial pilot. If you can include an SLA appendix draft (response tiers/credit triggers) that will speed procurement review.

We’ll upload the high-res log and the legal contact in HubSpot by EOD tomorrow.

Emily

---
(Forwarded attachment: nimble_sla_requirements.pdf)

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>

Date: Wed, 22 Feb 2028 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Internal: NimbleCart pilot owners & timeline

Quick internal note following Emily’s confirmation:
- Owners: Jordan (pilot proposal + sizing sweep), Marcus (cost estimate + SLA appendix), Ava (customer coordination + HubSpot updates).
- Deliverables by Thu EOD: 1) 2-page pilot recommendation, 2) rough cost delta hosted vs small Dedicated, 3) SLA appendix draft.
- Action: Jordan, run burst simulation once you ingest the 1-hour high-res log (Emily said they’ll upload tomorrow). Marcus, draft the credit triggers tied to 95/99 latency bands. I’ll open a HubSpot attachment entry and create JIRA ticket RED-7314 for the pilot.

Ava

Procurement note: we do have a flexible budget for promotional capacity but need an estimated monthly delta (ballpark) between hosted and a small Dedicated commit for initial pilot. If you can include an SLA appendix draft (response tiers/credit triggers) that will speed procurement review.

We’ll upload the high-res log and the legal contact in HubSpot by EOD tomorrow.

Emily

Ava
